command: $pre=(Get-FileHash 'C:\images\install.wim' -Algorithm SHA256).Hash; dism /Image:C:\mount /Add-Driver /Driver:C:\staging\maldrv.inf /ForceUnsigned | Out-Null; dism /Unmount-Image /MountDir:C:\mount /Commit | Out-Null; $post=(Get-FileHash 'C:\images\install.wim' -Algorithm SHA256).Hash; if($pre -ne $post){Write-Output 'WIM modified by driver injection'}; certutil -hashfile C:\images\install.wim SHA256
explanation: Adds an unsigned driver to the mounted image and commits the change, then verifies the WIM hash changed, demonstrating patching of the system image (T1601).
rationale_steps: Inject unsigned driver into the offline image with DISM.
Commit changes by unmounting the image.
Re-hash and compare to confirm image modification and log via certutil.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The PowerShell one-liner is well-formed with balanced quotes/parentheses, valid DISM flags, and correct use of separators and piping.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: It appropriately uses DISM to inject an unsigned driver into a mounted WIM and commit changes (aligning with T1601), but it assumes a pre-mounted image and requires administrative privileges despite claiming none.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is well-formed PowerShell with balanced parentheses and quotes, valid DISM flags, proper semicolon separators, and correct pipeline usage throughout.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command accurately demonstrates T1601 by capturing a pre-modification hash, injecting an unsigned driver into a mounted WIM image via DISM, committing the change, then verifying the hash changed and confirming with certutil — all operationally plausible steps for system image patching on Windows.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command follows proper PowerShell syntax, uses valid DISM flags, correctly chains commands with semicolons, and maintains balanced quoting.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command accurately implements the technique by modifying a Windows image with an unsigned driver, though it assumes the image is already mounted and requires administrative privileges to execute successfully.
